skill.name: notebooklm
skill.description: Query Google NotebookLM notebooks from local AI coding tools using browser automation, saved notebook metadata, and source-grounded answers.
skill.summary: Ask NotebookLM notebooks questions from Claude Code, Codex, or Claude with local browser automation.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis correctly identified a high-risk combination of scripts, network access, filesystem writes, browser session storage, and external commands. I found no evidence of prompt injection or third-party credential exfiltration, but the skill intentionally stores Google cookies and automates NotebookLM with anti-detection browser tooling, so it should not be published without human policy review.

high_findings.title: Persistent Google Browser Session Storage
high_findings.description: The skill intentionally saves Google NotebookLM browser state, including cookies and local storage, to disk for reuse. This is core functionality but creates high impact if the local skill directory is exposed or mishandled.
high_findings.recommendation: Require explicit user consent before authentication, document the stored cookie risk clearly, restrict file permissions, and provide a prominent cleanup command.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly calls storage_state to save cookies and documentation explicitly describes state.json as cookies plus localStorage. This confirms a real sensitive-data handling risk, even without evidence of exfiltration.

high_findings.title: Anti-Detection Browser Automation Against Google Service
high_findings.description: The skill uses Patchright and browser fingerprint consistency to automate NotebookLM. This may violate service expectations and increases account, compliance, and abuse risk for marketplace users.
high_findings.recommendation: Do not publish without policy review. If allowed, disclose the third-party automation behavior and require users to authenticate manually.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: Multiple files describe browser fingerprint consistency, Patchright setup, and manual cookie injection. The behavior is intentional and connected to a real external Google service.

medium_findings.title: Automatic Subprocess Execution and Dependency Installation
medium_findings.description: The runner creates a virtual environment, installs dependencies, installs a browser, and executes selected local Python scripts. Arguments are list-based and paths are constrained, but the behavior still executes code and network installation steps on the user machine.
medium_findings.recommendation: Keep script names constrained to the local scripts directory, avoid shell execution, pin dependencies, and ask users before first-time setup.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess calls are explicit and perform installation and execution. The implementation avoids shell=True and uses local paths, so this is elevated operational risk rather than confirmed command injection.

medium_findings.title: Local Data Deletion Utility
medium_findings.description: The cleanup manager can delete browser state, sessions, library data, authentication metadata, and other files under the skill data directory. The design includes preview and confirmation, reducing likelihood of accidental deletion.
medium_findings.recommendation: Keep deletion scoped to the skill data directory and preserve confirmation prompts unless the user passes an explicit force flag.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file deletion logic is present and uses shutil.rmtree and unlink. It appears scoped and user-confirmed, so it is a legitimate utility with moderate filesystem risk.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command Examples Misclassified as Ruby Backticks
low_findings.description: Many static external-command findings are Markdown code fences and inline shell examples in documentation, not Ruby execution inside application code. They are still operational instructions, but not hidden runtime behavior.
low_findings.recommendation: Treat documentation commands as user-visible setup instructions and ensure examples do not encourage unsafe shell usage.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are Markdown examples and instructions, not executable Ruby source. Static pattern matching likely interpreted backticks and fenced commands as code execution.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto and C2 Keyword Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Several blocker findings appear to match benign text such as NotebookLM, Gemini model descriptions, or example topic names. I did not find evidence that the skill implements cryptography or command-and-control behavior.
low_findings.recommendation: Dismiss these specific keyword hits unless future code introduces real cryptographic routines or suspicious network endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited contexts are product descriptions and user examples. No cryptographic implementation or C2-style endpoint was found in the reviewed files.

low_findings.title: Optional Environment Configuration Dependency
low_findings.description: The package includes python-dotenv and documents optional .env settings. I did not find evidence of secret exfiltration, but environment file support can expose local configuration if expanded later.
low_findings.recommendation: Document accepted environment keys and avoid loading or printing unrelated secrets from user environment files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The dependency and documentation are real, but reviewed code did not show broad environment secret collection. This is a low-confidence future misuse concern.

content.value_statement: Research answers often lose source context when documents live outside the coding workflow. This skill lets local AI tools ask NotebookLM notebooks and reuse source-grounded answers inside the terminal.
actual_capabilities: Checks and manages a local NotebookLM authentication state.
Asks questions against a selected NotebookLM notebook URL or saved notebook ID.
Maintains a local notebook library with names, topics, descriptions, and active notebook selection.
Uses browser automation to submit questions and capture NotebookLM answers.
Provides cleanup tools for browser state, auth metadata, and saved notebook data.
limitations: Requires local execution with Python, browser automation, and network access.
Requires manual Google login during authentication setup.
Stores browser cookies and local NotebookLM metadata on disk.
Depends on NotebookLM page structure and Google service limits.

use_cases.title: Ask project documentation questions
use_cases.description: Query a NotebookLM notebook that contains architecture notes, requirements, or internal docs before making implementation decisions.

use_cases.title: Build research summaries from source notebooks
use_cases.description: Use NotebookLM answers as grounded input for concise summaries, comparisons, and follow-up research planning.

use_cases.title: Organize recurring knowledge sources
use_cases.description: Save notebook metadata and activate the right NotebookLM notebook for repeated team or project workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Ask a basic notebook question
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner lookup from the active notebook.
prompt_templates.prompt: Ask my active NotebookLM notebook: What are the main requirements for this project? Summarize the answer with citations when available.

prompt_templates.title: Add and describe a notebook
prompt_templates.prompt: Add this NotebookLM URL to my library. First inspect the notebook content, then save a clear name, description, and topic list.

prompt_templates.title: Compare source-backed options
prompt_templates.prompt: Ask NotebookLM which implementation options are supported by the sources. Compare the options, list tradeoffs, and identify missing information.

prompt_templates.title: Run multi-step source research
prompt_templates.prompt: Use NotebookLM to answer this research question. Ask follow-up questions until the answer covers evidence, risks, assumptions, and open gaps.

output_examples.input: Ask the active notebook what migration risks are documented.
output_examples.output: A concise answer listing the documented migration risks, source-backed details, and follow-up questions for missing areas.

output_examples.input: Search my notebook library for onboarding materials.
output: A short description of each match.
The active notebook status when relevant.

output_examples.input: Add this NotebookLM URL and infer its topic list.
output_examples.output: A saved notebook entry with a descriptive name, content summary, topic tags, and confirmation that it can be activated later.

best_practices: Authenticate only on a trusted local machine and clean browser state when access is no longer needed.
Use notebook IDs and clear descriptions so future queries target the right source collection.
Ask focused follow-up questions because each answer depends on the notebook content and current page behavior.
anti_patterns: Do not use this skill in shared or untrusted environments where browser cookies can be copied.
Do not treat NotebookLM answers as complete when the uploaded sources are incomplete or outdated.
Do not publish automation workflows without reviewing Google account, service, and data handling requirements.

faq.question: Does this skill work without a Google account?
faq.answer: No. It requires manual Google login to access NotebookLM notebooks.

faq.question: Where does it store notebook and auth data?
faq.answer: It stores local data in the skill directory, including notebook metadata and browser state files.

faq.question: Can it upload documents to NotebookLM?
faq.answer: No. The documented workflow assumes users create notebooks and upload sources in NotebookLM first.

faq.question: Can it be used from Codex?
faq.answer: The metadata lists Codex support, but the instructions are written primarily for local Claude Code style execution.

faq.question: Why is this skill marked high risk?
faq.answer: It stores Google browser session data, installs dependencies, and automates an external web service.

faq.question: How can users remove saved authentication data?
faq.answer: The included cleanup and auth commands can clear browser state and authentication metadata from the local skill data directory.
